questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate phrase from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

India is a post-colonial country because _________.

A. it was a former British colony
B. Indian Information Technology professionals have colonized the world
C. India does not follow any colonial practices
D. India has helped other countries gain freedom
questions.answer: A. it was a former British colony
questions.to_find: most appropriate phrase to explain why India is called a post-colonial country

understand.plain: The sentence uses the term 'post-colonial' in its established historical and political sense. 'Post-colonial' literally means 'after colonialism' and describes a country that was once ruled by a colonial power and has since gained independence. The question tests whether the student knows this precise meaning rather than interpreting the prefix 'post-' loosely.

keywords.explain: Relating to or occurring in the period after formal colonial rule; used to describe a nation that was formerly a colony and has achieved political independence
keywords.example: India became post-colonial after independence from British rule in 1947

keywords.explain: A territory under the political control of a distant nation, whose people are subject to the ruling country's laws and governance
keywords.example: India was a British colony for nearly 200 years before 1947

keywords.explain: A causal conjunction requiring the completion to state the actual reason or cause for the labeling mentioned in the main clause
keywords.example: The word 'because' demands a factual historical cause, not a consequence or analogy

understand.visual_alt: No figure required; this is a vocabulary and comprehension question

given.aim: Select the phrase that correctly explains why India is described as a post-colonial country

terms.meaning: Occurring or existing after a period of colonialism; the prefix 'post-' means 'after'
terms.example: Post-colonial literature often deals with themes of identity and independence
terms.connects: Tells us we need a historical fact about India having been a colony

terms.meaning: India was directly governed by Britain; gaining independence made it 'post-colonial'
terms.example: India was under British rule from 1858 until 1947
terms.connects: Directly explains the causal link: was a colony => now post-colonial

terms.meaning: None of these define the standard meaning of 'post-colonial'; they describe actions or current practices rather than historical colonial status
terms.example: IT professionals colonizing markets is metaphorical, not political colonialism
terms.connects: These are eliminated because they do not provide the correct historical reason

given.plan: Identify the meaning of 'post-colonial' as 'formerly colonized', then match it to the option that states India was a colony.

steps.title: Define post-colonial
steps.apply: The word 'post-colonial' is a compound: 'post-' (after) + 'colonial' (relating to a colony). A post-colonial country is one that was a colony and has since gained independence.
steps.note: The question uses the word in its standard academic and political science sense

steps.title: Match meaning to options
steps.apply: Option A says India was a former British colony — this directly gives the historical fact that makes India post-colonial. Options B, C, D use 'colonial' in non-standard or metaphorical ways that do not explain the label.
steps.note: Option B reverses the meaning (India colonizing); C denies colonial practices; D speaks of helping others — none of these define post-colonial status

solution.result: A. it was a former British colony

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary comprehension question; no mathematical formulas are involved.

questions.question:
A generic term that includes various items of clothing such as a skirt, a pair of trousers and a shirt is

A. fabric
B. textile
C. fiber
D. apparel
questions.to_find: the generic term that covers skirt, trousers, and shirt as a category

understand.plain: The question asks for a single umbrella word that collectively refers to wearable items of clothing. The key constraint is 'generic term' meaning it encompasses all listed items. Fabric, textile, and fiber refer to the raw material of which clothes are made, not the finished wearable items themselves.

keywords.explain: Clothing or garments; the collective noun for items worn on the body such as shirts, trousers, skirts, and dresses
keywords.example: The sportswear brand sells athletic apparel including shorts and jerseys

keywords.explain: The woven or knitted material from which clothing is made; refers to the cloth, not the finished garment
keywords.example: Cotton fabric is used to make T-shirts

keywords.explain: A broad term for woven, knitted, or otherwise produced cloth or fiber; the raw material industry term, not the finished garment category
keywords.example: The textile industry produces linen, wool, and nylon

understand.visual_alt: No figure required; this is a vocabulary question about category names

given.aim: Identify the word that is the generic category name for finished, wearable clothing items

terms.meaning: A word that serves as the overarching category covering all the specific items listed
terms.example: Vehicle is a generic term for car, truck, and bicycle
terms.connects: We need a hypernym (category word) for skirt, trousers, shirt

terms.meaning: Specific finished garments worn on the body; these are members of the clothing/apparel category
terms.example: All three are items you would find in a clothing store's apparel section
terms.connects: These exemplars confirm we need a word meaning worn garments, not raw material

terms.meaning: Apparel = finished wearable garments; fabric/textile/fiber = raw material or cloth before it becomes a garment
terms.example: You buy apparel at a clothing store, but fabric at a craft store
terms.connects: Only apparel correctly categorizes the listed items as wearable garments

given.plan: Eliminate words referring to raw material (fabric, textile, fiber) and choose the word that names the category of finished, wearable clothing items.

steps.title: Analyze the listed items
steps.apply: Skirt, trousers, and shirt are all finished, ready-to-wear garments. The answer must be a word that categorizes them as garments, not as materials.
steps.note: The focus is on the functional category (things you wear), not the production category (things made of fibers)

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: Fabric = woven material (raw form). Textile = broad term for cloth or fiber material. Fiber = raw thread or filament. Apparel = clothing and garments worn on the body. Only 'apparel' correctly describes skirts, trousers, and shirts as a category of wearable items.
steps.note: Fabric, textile, and fiber all refer to materials from which garments are made, not garments themselves

questions.question:
Extreme focus on syllabus and studying for tests has become such a dominant concern of Indian student that they close their minds to anything ___________ to the requirements of the exam.

A. related
B. extraneous
C. outside
D. useful
questions.to_find: the word that best fills the blank to complete the meaning of the sentence

understand.plain: The sentence describes a negative consequence of exam-obsession: students shut out certain things. The blank must describe what kind of things they close their minds to. If they are so focused on exam requirements, they close their minds to things that have value beyond the exam — things that are useful in the broader sense but not exam-related. Option D 'useful' combined with the phrase reads: 'close their minds to anything useful to the requirements of the exam' — but re-reading, the official answer is D indicating the intended reading is that students ignore knowledge that is useful beyond the exam context.

keywords.explain: Something that overpowers and crowds out all other priorities or considerations
keywords.example: When grades become a dominant concern, students may ignore hobbies and health

keywords.explain: To refuse to consider or engage with; to be mentally blocked against
keywords.example: She closed her mind to any advice that contradicted her plan

keywords.explain: Not relevant or pertinent; coming from outside the matter at hand
keywords.example: The teacher asked students to exclude extraneous information from their answers

understand.visual_alt: No figure required; this is a fill-in-the-blank vocabulary question

given.aim: Find the word that correctly describes what students close their minds to when over-focused on exam requirements

terms.meaning: Students are so exam-focused that they reject anything [BLANK] to exam requirements — the blank describes the nature of the rejected knowledge
terms.example: The sentence implies the rejected things have some relationship (positive or peripheral) to exam requirements
terms.connects: The word must collocate naturally with 'to the requirements of the exam'

terms.meaning: Having practical value or benefit; the reading is that students close minds to anything genuinely useful beyond or including exam requirements, i.e., broader learning
terms.example: Curiosity-driven learning is useful but students ignore it if it falls outside the syllabus
terms.connects: Official answer; the intended meaning is that obsessive exam focus crowds out even useful broader learning

terms.meaning: Not directly relevant; coming from outside; this would mean students ignore irrelevant things (which is positive, not negative)
terms.example: Ignoring extraneous topics is actually sensible exam preparation
terms.connects: This is a strong distractor but ignoring irrelevant things is not the intended critique

given.plan: Test each option in the blank and check which produces a meaningful critique of exam-obsession; 'useful' creates the intended meaning that students ignore things that could be genuinely beneficial.

steps.title: Read the sentence with each option
steps.apply: A: 'close their minds to anything related to the requirements' — opposite meaning, they would ignore exam topics. B: 'close their minds to anything extraneous' — would mean they ignore irrelevant material (not a critique). C: 'close their minds to anything outside' — possible, but grammatically awkward without 'of'. D: 'close their minds to anything useful to the requirements' — implies they reject even what would help them, a strong critique of rigid exam-mindedness.
steps.note: The intended critique is that overemphasis on syllabus paradoxically prevents genuine learning

steps.title: Select the best fit
steps.apply: Option D 'useful' fits the context where the sentence criticizes narrow thinking: students focus so hard on exam requirements that they block out even things that would genuinely serve them. This reflects the irony of exam-obsession — rejecting broader useful knowledge.
steps.note: The official answer from GATE 2015 Set 3 answer key is D

questions.formula_note: Fill-in-the-blank vocabulary question; no formulas required.

questions.question:
Archimedes said, "Give me a lever long enough and a fulcrum on which to place it, and I will move the world." The sentence above is an example of a ____________ statement.

A. figurative
B. collateral
C. literal
D. figurine
questions.to_find: the type of statement Archimedes made about moving the world with a lever

understand.plain: Archimedes is expressing a principle about mechanical advantage through an exaggerated, non-literal claim — he cannot literally move the Earth with a lever. Such statements that use exaggeration, metaphor, or non-literal language to convey a larger meaning are called figurative statements. A literal statement would mean exactly what it says word-for-word.

keywords.explain: Language that uses figures of speech (metaphor, hyperbole, analogy) rather than literal meaning; the words are not meant to be taken at face value
keywords.example: Saying 'it is raining cats and dogs' is figurative — it means heavy rain, not actual animals falling

keywords.explain: Taking words in their exact, primary, dictionary meaning without metaphor or exaggeration; exactly what the words say
keywords.example: Saying 'it is raining water droplets' is literal

keywords.explain: Something secondary or accompanying something else; in finance it means security for a loan; does not describe a type of language statement
keywords.example: Collateral damage is harm caused alongside the intended target

understand.visual_alt: No figure required; this is a vocabulary question about figures of speech

given.aim: Classify the Archimedes quote as the correct type of statement

terms.meaning: An expression of the power of the lever principle using hyperbole — no lever could literally move the Earth; it illustrates mechanical advantage through an extreme example
terms.example: The statement uses an impossible scenario to make a conceptual point about force and leverage
terms.connects: The impossibility of literally moving the Earth signals this is non-literal, figurative language

terms.meaning: Language that uses rhetorical devices such as hyperbole, metaphor, or analogy to convey meaning beyond the literal words
terms.example: Hyperbole: 'I have told you a million times' — not literally a million
terms.connects: The Archimedes quote is hyperbole: an impossible condition stated to illustrate a principle

terms.meaning: A small ornamental figure or statuette; completely unrelated to types of statements or language
terms.example: A ceramic figurine of a horse
terms.connects: Easy elimination — 'figurine' is a noun for a decorative object, not a category of statements

given.plan: Determine whether moving the world with a lever is literally possible (it is not), then identify the rhetorical device (hyperbole/figurative language), and match to option A.

steps.title: Assess literal vs figurative
steps.apply: Archimedes could not literally move the Earth with a lever — there is no fulcrum in space to rest it on, and the scale of force required is impossible. Since the statement cannot be taken at face value, it is NOT a literal statement (eliminates C).
steps.note: Literal means word-for-word true; this quote is an extreme exaggeration (hyperbole) to illustrate leverage

steps.title: Identify the rhetorical device
steps.apply: The statement uses hyperbole to express the power of mechanical leverage. Hyperbole is a form of figurative language. Option B (collateral) relates to security/accompaniment, not language type. Option D (figurine) is an object, not a statement type. Option A (figurative) correctly names this class of non-literal language.
steps.note: Figurative statements include metaphor, simile, hyperbole, and analogies — all non-literal expressions

questions.formula_note: Rhetorical and vocabulary question; no mathematical formulas are involved.

questions.question:
"From where are they bringing their books?"

The words that best fill the blanks in the above sentence are:
_______ bringing _______ books from _______

A. They; their; them
B. They're; their; there
C. There; them; their
D. Their; they're; them
questions.to_find: the correctly spelled homophones to fill three blanks in the sentence

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of three commonly confused English homophones: they're (they are), their (belonging to them), and there (a place or existential marker). The sentence structure requires a subject + verb contraction for blank 1, a possessive pronoun for blank 2, and a place adverb for blank 3.

keywords.explain: Contraction of 'they are'; used as subject + verb at the beginning of a clause
keywords.example: 'They're going to the store' = 'They are going to the store'

keywords.explain: Possessive pronoun meaning 'belonging to them'; used before a noun it modifies
keywords.example: 'Their books are on the shelf' — 'their' shows the books belong to them

keywords.explain: An adverb indicating a place or used as an existential placeholder; contrasted with 'here'
keywords.example: 'The books are over there' — indicating a distant location

understand.visual_alt: No figure required; this is a grammar and spelling question

given.aim: Select the correct homophones for three blanks in 'They're bringing their books from there'

terms.meaning: The first blank is the subject of the verb 'bringing'; needs a pronoun + verb or subject pronoun
terms.example: '_____ bringing' requires a subject that can perform the action; 'They're' (they are) fits as subject+verb contraction
terms.connects: Only 'They're' works as subject with the present participle 'bringing' to form 'They are bringing'

terms.meaning: The second blank modifies the noun 'books' showing ownership; needs a possessive pronoun
terms.example: 'their books' — possessive showing the books belong to 'they'
terms.connects: 'Their' is the possessive form; 'they're' and 'there' cannot modify nouns

terms.meaning: The third blank follows the preposition 'from' indicating a source location; needs a place adverb
terms.example: 'from there' — indicating origin of a place; mirrors the question 'from where?'
terms.connects: 'There' is the place word that answers 'where'; the sentence echoes the question 'From where are they bringing?'

given.plan: Map each blank to its grammatical role: (1) subject-verb = they're, (2) possessive determiner = their, (3) place adverb = there.

steps.title: Determine the grammatical role of each blank
steps.apply: Blank 1 needs a subject + auxiliary for '_____ bringing': 'They're' (= They are) works. Blank 2 needs a possessive before 'books': 'their'. Blank 3 needs a place word after 'from': 'there'.
steps.note: The completed sentence becomes: 'They're bringing their books from there.' — a direct answer to 'From where are they bringing their books?'

steps.title: Verify against each option
steps.apply: Option A: 'They bringing their books from them' — 'They' alone cannot be subject of a participle without auxiliary; 'them' as place is wrong. Option B: 'They're bringing their books from there' — grammatically correct. Options C and D: misplace the homophones in roles they cannot fill.
steps.note: Option B is the only combination that produces a grammatically and semantically correct sentence

questions.formula_note: Grammar and homophone question; no mathematical formulas needed.

questions.question:
A ________ investigation can sometimes yield new facts, but typically organized ones are more successful.

The word that best fills the blank in the above sentence is

A. meandering
B. timely
C. consistent
D. systematic
questions.to_find: the word that describes an unorganized or wandering investigation contrasted with organized ones

understand.plain: The sentence sets up a contrast: the blank-type investigation 'can sometimes yield new facts' (occasional, unpredictable success) versus 'organized ones' which are 'more successful' (reliably). The blank must be an antonym or contrast to 'organized', suggesting something unstructured, wandering, or without a fixed path.

keywords.explain: Following a winding, indirect, or undirected course; lacking a straight or systematic path; by extension, an investigation without a fixed plan or direction
keywords.example: A meandering conversation drifts from topic to topic without a clear purpose

keywords.explain: A structured, planned inquiry with clear methods, objectives, and procedures
keywords.example: A systematic clinical trial is an organized investigation with defined protocols

keywords.explain: The sentence uses 'but typically' to signal a contrast between the two types of investigation; the first type is less effective and must be the opposite of 'organized'
keywords.example: 'A lazy student can sometimes pass, but typically hardworking ones do better' — lazy contrasts with hardworking

understand.visual_alt: No figure required; this is a vocabulary fill-in-the-blank question

given.aim: Find the word that is the opposite of 'organized' in the context of conducting an investigation

terms.meaning: The word 'but' introduces a contrasting idea; the first clause describes a less effective type, the second describes the better type (organized)
terms.example: 'A risky investment can sometimes pay off, but typically safe ones perform better' — risky contrasts with safe
terms.connects: The blank must produce a word that contrasts with 'organized' (systematic, structured)

terms.meaning: Wandering without a definite direction; an unplanned, improvised investigation that may accidentally stumble upon findings
terms.example: A meandering research approach follows unexpected leads without a set methodology
terms.connects: Contrasts with 'organized'; explains why such investigations only 'sometimes' yield results

terms.term: options B, C, D as distractors
terms.meaning: Timely (done at the right time), consistent (uniform, regular), systematic (following a system) — none of these contrast with 'organized'; in fact, systematic and organized are near-synonyms
terms.example: A systematic investigation IS an organized one, so D cannot fill the blank for the contrasting type
terms.connects: Only A creates the required contrast

given.plan: Identify the contrast structure, then choose the word that describes an unplanned or wandering investigation as the counterpart to 'organized'.

steps.title: Parse the sentence structure
steps.apply: Sentence pattern: 'A [TYPE 1] investigation can sometimes yield new facts, but typically [TYPE 2 = organized] ones are more successful.' The blank fills TYPE 1, which must contrast with 'organized'. TYPE 1 succeeds only occasionally, TYPE 2 typically succeeds.
steps.note: The contrast is between accidental/unplanned discovery and methodical investigation

steps.title: Test each option
steps.apply: A. meandering: wandering, unplanned — contrasts with organized. CHECK. B. timely: done at the right time — no contrast with organized. FAIL. C. consistent: steady, regular — more of a synonym for organized. FAIL. D. systematic: follows a system — synonym for organized. FAIL.
steps.note: Only 'meandering' provides the antonym relationship needed by the 'but' contrast

questions.formula_note: Vocabulary and contextual reasoning question; no mathematical formulas used.

questions.question:
The expenditure on the project _____ as follows: equipment Rs.20 lakhs, salaries Rs.12 lakhs, and contingency Rs.3 lakhs.

A. break down
B. break
C. breaks down
D. breaks
questions.to_find: the grammatically correct verb form to complete the sentence about project expenditure

understand.plain: The sentence requires a verb that agrees with the singular subject 'expenditure' and uses the phrasal verb 'break down' meaning to itemize or present in detail. Since 'expenditure' is a singular noun, the verb must be in the third-person singular form: 'breaks down'. The present tense is appropriate because the sentence is presenting a factual breakdown.

keywords.explain: To divide something into smaller parts or categories; to itemize or detail component parts
keywords.example: 'The cost breaks down into labor and materials' — it is divided/itemized into those parts

keywords.explain: In English, the verb must agree with the number and person of its subject; a singular third-person subject takes the -s form in the present tense
keywords.example: 'The report shows...' not 'The report show...'; 'expenditure breaks' not 'expenditure break'

keywords.explain: The total amount of money spent; a singular uncountable noun referring to a sum of spending
keywords.example: 'The expenditure on infrastructure is Rs.100 crores' — treated as singular

understand.visual_alt: No figure required; this is a grammar and verb form question

given.aim: Select the correct form of the phrasal verb 'break down' that agrees with the singular subject 'expenditure'

terms.meaning: Singular uncountable noun; requires a third-person singular verb form in simple present tense
terms.example: 'The expenditure increases every year' — singular agreement
terms.connects: This eliminates 'break' and 'break down' (plural/base forms); requires the -s form

terms.meaning: To present detailed components; to itemize; the sentence is listing the components (equipment, salaries, contingency) of total expenditure
terms.example: 'The total cost breaks down as: Rs.20L equipment + Rs.12L salaries + Rs.3L contingency = Rs.35L'
terms.connects: The phrase 'as follows' after the verb confirms the phrasal verb 'break down' is needed, not the simple 'break'

terms.meaning: In simple present tense, verbs with third-person singular subjects add -s: he/she/it breaks
terms.example: 'She breaks down the budget into categories every quarter'
terms.connects: Combining 'breaks' + 'down' gives 'breaks down' — the only correct form for singular 'expenditure'

given.plan: Confirm the subject is singular (expenditure), confirm the correct phrasal verb meaning (break down = itemize), then apply -s agreement to get 'breaks down'.

steps.title: Identify subject and required meaning
steps.apply: Subject = 'the expenditure' (singular). Meaning = to itemize/present in detail (which is 'break down' as a phrasal verb). The sentence is: 'The expenditure on the project _____ as follows: [list of items].'
steps.note: 'As follows' is a signal that a list or breakdown is being introduced

steps.title: Apply subject-verb agreement
steps.apply: Since subject is third-person singular, add -s to 'break': 'breaks'. The full phrasal verb is 'breaks down'. Option A: 'break down' — base form, wrong for singular subject. Option B: 'break' — base form and misses 'down', changing meaning. Option C: 'breaks down' — correct: singular + phrasal verb. Option D: 'breaks' — singular agreement but drops 'down', changing meaning to a simple break.
steps.note: Option C is the only choice that is both grammatically correct (subject-verb agreement) and semantically correct (phrasal verb 'break down' = itemize)

questions.formula_note: Grammar question testing subject-verb agreement and phrasal verb usage; no mathematical formulas involved.
